worth_it_explanation: Amazon audiences are the engine of Amazon DSP's differentiation. In-market, lifestyle, and remarketing segments built from Amazon shopping signals give endemic advertisers reach into intent that no third-party data provider can replicate. Misconfiguring them, whether by stacking audiences so broadly they dilute relevance or using remarketing lookbacks that no longer match real purchase cycles, bleeds budget toward people who already bought or people who were never close to buying. These checks are fast reads inside the Amazon DSP console and clarify whether the audience setup actually matches the funnel stage each line item is meant to reach.
intro: Amazon DSP audiences are built from Amazon's first-party shopping graph, which encompasses search queries, product detail page views, purchases, and category browsing across Amazon.com and affiliated properties. Three audience types are available to advertisers: in-market segments (shoppers showing recent purchase intent in a category), lifestyle segments (shoppers with demonstrated long-term affinity), and remarketing audiences (shoppers who viewed or purchased specific Amazon Standard Identification Numbers, or ASINs). These checks examine whether those audiences are configured with the intent signals, lookback windows, and funnel alignment that make them worth paying for.

audit_checks.title: Amazon audiences are stacked too broadly or without intent layering
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Broad audience stacks reach people far from a purchase decision. CPMs rise because more inventory qualifies, frequency accumulates against low-intent users, and the measured return on ad spend (ROAS) drops as the audience pool dilutes the signal that Amazon shopping data provides.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP lets advertisers build audiences from in-market segments, lifestyle segments, and remarketing lists, then layer them at the line item level. The value of in-market audiences comes from recency: they identify shoppers who browsed or searched within a category in the past 30 days or fewer. Lifestyle audiences capture longer-term affinity, typically 90 days or more. When advertisers stack all three types without intent layering, for example adding a lifestyle audience alongside an in-market audience without restricting to users who also viewed a relevant product, the effective reach expands well beyond the intent-rich core the higher CPM is meant to capture.

The practical consequence is frequency distributed across a population that spans shoppers who are ready to buy, shoppers who bought six months ago, and shoppers who once browsed an adjacent category. Amazon's auction CPMs reflect audience quality, so a broader stack raises clearing prices while reducing the share of impressions reaching the highest-intent users.

A few signs point to this problem at the line item level:
  • The line item carries three or more audience segments that differ in recency, with no exclusion or intersection logic connecting them
  • Lifestyle segments and in-market segments cover the same broad category without a product-specific remarketing layer to anchor intent
  • The audience size in the Amazon DSP console is an order of magnitude larger than the brand's realistic addressable market for the campaign flight
You may want to review the audience composition for each line item and confirm that the segments in use match the funnel stage the line item is designed to reach. A prospecting line item can tolerate a broader lifestyle audience; a retargeting line item should use product-specific ASIN remarketing or in-market audiences with short lookbacks.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers selling products on Amazon, the audience layering question connects directly to the Amazon Standard Identification Number (ASIN). A line item targeting an in-market segment for 'skin care' without also layering ASIN viewers or cart abandoners for the brand's own products reaches a much wider pool than a line item that intersects both. You may want to confirm that prospecting and retargeting line items use distinctly different audience configurations, and that retargeting line items anchor to product-specific remarketing rather than relying on category-level segments.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers driving to off-Amazon destinations, the product-level ASIN layer is not available. The relevant question is whether in-market segments match the advertiser's actual category. A financial services advertiser using a broad lifestyle segment for 'business owners' reaches a different audience than one using in-market segments for financial products. You may want to confirm the segments in use reflect genuine purchase-intent signals relevant to the advertised product, not the nearest available Amazon category approximation.

learn_more.source_title: Set up audience targeting on Amazon DSP

investigate.intro: Review the audience configuration for each active line item in the Amazon DSP console, confirming that segments are layered with intent rather than simply stacked, and that the audience size is consistent with the campaign's goal.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the order and navigate to each active line item.

In the line item's targeting settings, open the audience section and note every Amazon audience segment applied. Record whether the line item uses in-market segments, lifestyle segments, ASIN remarketing, or a combination.

For each line item with more than one audience type, check whether an intersection or exclusion is applied. A line item that ANDs an in-market segment with an ASIN remarketing list is narrower and higher-intent than one that ORs them.

Note the estimated audience size the console reports. Compare it against the campaign's realistic addressable market. A size that is one or two orders of magnitude larger than you would expect suggests the segments are set to OR logic or cover a broader category than intended.
Record any line item where a lifestyle segment and an in-market segment are both active without a product-specific layer, and flag it as a candidate for narrowing.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per line item, the audience types in use, whether intersection or exclusion logic is applied, and the estimated audience size. A line item that uses segments matched to its funnel stage, with intersection logic connecting broad and narrow segments where applicable, passes. A line item that stacks lifestyle and in-market segments without an anchoring product-level layer, or whose estimated audience size is implausibly large for the campaign goal, is flagged with the line item name and the segment combination observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the audience configuration and estimated size for each line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that several line items in {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP order stack audience segments without intent layering, reaching a broader pool than the campaign goal warrants. In-market and lifestyle segments are applied with OR logic rather than intersection, which distributes impressions across shoppers at very different stages of the purchase cycle and dilutes the efficiency advantage that Amazon's shopping signals are meant to provide. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the audience configuration for each affected line item, applying intersection logic where a product-specific ASIN layer or a shorter-lookback in-market segment can anchor the audience to higher-intent users, and confirming that the estimated audience size in the Amazon DSP console is consistent with the campaign's addressable market.

audit_checks.title: Remarketing audience lookback windows do not match the product's purchase cycle
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A lookback window that is too long includes people who viewed the product weeks before their intent lapsed. A lookback that is too short misses a large share of the potential retarget pool. Either misconfiguration means spend goes toward the wrong users or the audience is too small to deliver efficiently.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP remarketing audiences are built from shoppers who viewed or purchased specific ASINs within a configurable lookback window. The default options Amazon provides range from 7 days to 365 days depending on the audience type. A 7-day ASIN viewer audience captures shoppers who are still in an active consideration window; a 90-day viewer audience includes people whose intent may have resolved, either through purchase elsewhere or through loss of interest.

The right lookback depends on how long the purchase decision typically takes for the product category. A household consumable that shoppers reorder monthly has a short natural cycle. A consumer electronics item that shoppers research for two to three weeks has a medium cycle. A mattress or appliance may warrant a longer window because the decision can stretch across several months.

When the lookback is set longer than the typical decision cycle, the remarketing audience accumulates users who viewed the product once, resolved their decision weeks ago, and are no longer in-market. Impressions delivered to this group spend budget on people with low incremental probability of converting. When the lookback is too short, the audience is smaller than it needs to be, which raises CPMs and can cause the line item to underdeliver.

A few signs point to a lookback mismatch:
  • A fast-moving consumer goods (FMCG) line item uses a 90-day or longer lookback, pulling in viewers whose consideration window closed long before the ad reaches them
  • A considered purchase category uses a 7-day lookback, excluding the majority of the potential retarget pool who are still deciding
  • Frequency capping on the remarketing line item is set so high that the same users see the ad repeatedly across a long lookback window, which accumulates wasted impressions late in a stale audience
You may want to confirm the lookback on each remarketing line item against the typical purchase cycle for the product category, and adjust where there is a clear mismatch.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, the product category is a reliable guide to the purchase cycle. ASIN remarketing for consumables sold through Subscribe and Save on Amazon typically warrants a lookback of 14 to 30 days. ASIN remarketing for electronics or home goods may warrant 30 to 60 days. You may want to check whether the current lookback reflects the category's typical decision timeline or whether it was left at the platform default without adjustment.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers using the Amazon Ad Tag pixel to build off-Amazon remarketing audiences, the lookback question applies to the page visit audience rather than an ASIN. A user who visited a financial services landing page six months ago represents a different retarget value than one who visited last week. You may want to confirm the pixel-based audience lookback matches the advertiser's typical lead nurture cycle rather than defaulting to the longest available window.

learn_more.source_title: A complete guide to Amazon Marketing Cloud

investigate.intro: Open each remarketing line item in the Amazon DSP console, record the lookback window configured for each ASIN or pixel audience, and compare it against the purchase cycle for the product category.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the order and navigate to each line item that uses a remarketing audience, as opposed to in-market or lifestyle segments.
In the line item's audience settings, find the ASIN viewer, ASIN purchaser, or pixel-based audience and note the lookback window configured.
For each remarketing line item, record the product category it serves and estimate the typical purchase decision cycle for that category.
Flag any line item where the lookback window is more than twice the typical decision cycle, or where it is shorter than the minimum research period the category requires.

Note any remarketing line item whose frequency cap is set higher than three to four impressions per user per day within the lookback window, as this can drive wasted impressions against a stale audience population.

amazon_marketing_cloud.label: Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC)
steps: If Amazon Marketing Cloud (AMC) access is available, run a path-to-conversion query to see how many days typically elapse between the first DSP impression and a purchase for each product category.
Compare the observed conversion window from AMC against the remarketing lookback configured in the console. A large gap between the two is evidence of a lookback mismatch.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per remarketing line item, the lookback window and the product category's typical purchase cycle. A lookback that falls within one to two times the typical decision cycle passes. A lookback that is more than twice the typical decision cycle, or shorter than the minimum research period the category requires, is flagged with the line item name and the specific mismatch observed.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the lookback window and the purchase cycle estimate for each remarketing line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the remarketing audiences in {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP order use lookback windows that do not align with the purchase cycles of the products being advertised. Several line items pull in ASIN viewers from windows that extend well past the period when those users were actively deciding, which directs spend toward an audience whose consideration window has closed. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the lookback configuration for each remarketing line item, aligning each window to the product category's typical decision timeline, and, where Amazon Marketing Cloud access is available, using path-to-conversion data to calibrate the window against observed behavior rather than a default setting.

audit_checks.title: Audience definitions do not match the funnel stage of the line item
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A prospecting line item that runs ASIN remarketing audiences, or a retargeting line item that runs only lifestyle segments, conflates funnel stages within a single order structure. The result is unclear attribution, frequency overlap across line items, and difficulty reading campaign performance by funnel stage.

educate.base:
Amazon DSP orders are structured as Advertiser > Order > Line Item > Creative. A well-organized order separates line items by funnel stage so that prospecting, consideration, and retargeting each operate with distinct audiences, bids, and creative. When line items mix audience types across funnel stages, for example using a lifestyle audience on a line item named 'Retargeting' or running ASIN remarketing on a line item named 'Awareness', the order structure stops reflecting actual targeting intent.

The practical problems this creates include attribution ambiguity, where a purchase attributed to the retargeting line item may have come from an audience the brand had not previously touched, and frequency overlap, where the same user appears in both the prospecting and retargeting audiences and accumulates frequency across both line items without any deduplication.

Amazon DSP does not automatically deduplicate audiences across line items within the same order. If a user qualifies for both a lifestyle segment on a prospecting line item and an ASIN remarketing list on a retargeting line item, they are eligible for impressions from both, which can drive total frequency higher than intended.

A few signs point to funnel-stage misalignment:
  • A line item named or tagged as 'Prospecting' or 'Awareness' uses an ASIN viewer or purchaser remarketing audience as its primary segment
  • A line item named or tagged as 'Retargeting' uses only broad lifestyle segments without a product-specific layer
  • Two line items in the same order use audiences with significant overlap, with no exclusion applied on the prospecting line item to remove users already in the retargeting pool
You may want to audit the audience-to-line-item mapping across the order and confirm that prospecting line items exclude current ASIN viewers or purchasers, and that retargeting line items use product-specific audiences rather than category-level ones.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: For endemic advertisers, the clearest version of funnel-stage alignment is: prospecting line items use in-market or lifestyle segments with an exclusion for recent ASIN viewers; consideration line items use ASIN viewers with longer lookbacks; retargeting line items use recent ASIN viewers, cart abandoners, or purchasers from related ASINs. You may want to confirm each line item in the order maps to one of these tiers and that the exclusions at the prospecting level prevent audience overlap with the retargeting pool.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: For non-endemic advertisers, the analog is: prospecting line items use in-market segments relevant to the advertised service; retargeting line items use pixel-based audiences of page visitors or form starters. You may want to confirm the prospecting line item excludes pixel-based retarget lists so the same user is not served both a prospecting and a retargeting impression in the same day.

learn_more.source_title: Set up audience targeting on Amazon DSP

investigate.intro: Map each line item in the order to a funnel stage based on its name or goal, then compare that stage against the audience type it uses and check for missing exclusions.

amazon_dsp_console.label: Amazon DSP console
steps: Open the order and list every active line item. For each, note the name or label and the intended goal, whether awareness, consideration, or retargeting.
Open each line item's audience settings and record the audience type: in-market, lifestyle, ASIN viewer, ASIN purchaser, pixel-based, or custom.

For each line item designated as prospecting or awareness, check whether an exclusion is applied to remove current ASIN viewers or existing customers from the audience. If no exclusion exists, the prospecting line item is reaching users who are already in the retargeting pool.
For each line item designated as retargeting, confirm it uses a product-specific audience rather than a broad category-level lifestyle or in-market segment.
Flag any line item where the audience type does not match the stated funnel stage or where exclusions that would prevent overlap with other line items are absent.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per line item, the stated funnel stage, the audience type used, and whether exclusions are in place to prevent overlap with other line items. A line item whose audience type matches its funnel stage and whose prospecting line items exclude retarget pools passes. A line item where the audience type conflicts with the stated stage, or where a prospecting line item has no exclusion for current ASIN viewers, is flagged with the line item name and the specific mismatch.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the funnel stage, audience type, and exclusion status for each line item above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that the audience definitions across {{site_name}}'s Amazon DSP order do not consistently match the funnel stage each line item is designed to reach. Several line items mix audience types in ways that create overlap between the prospecting and retargeting pools, and exclusions that would prevent the same user from qualifying for both are absent. This inflates effective frequency for overlapping users and makes it difficult to read performance cleanly by funnel stage. {{voice_recommend}} mapping each line item to a single funnel tier, aligning the audience type to that tier, and adding exclusions on prospecting line items to remove users already in the retargeting pool.
